coredata.dc:title: Quality by design−aided acid-free synthesis of self P, N, S-doped black seed-derived carbon quantum dots for application as a nanosensor for eltrombopag environmental and bioanalysis and pharmacokinetic assay
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Abd Elhaleem, Shymaa M.
Belal, Fathalla
El-Shabrawy, Yasser
El-Maghrabey, Mahmoud
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we developed a rapid, one-step, and cost-effective methodology based on the fabrication of water-soluble self-nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus co-doped black seed carbon quantum dots (BSQDs) via microwaveirradiation in six minutes. Our synthesis approach is superior to those in the literature as they involved long-time heating (12 h) with sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide and/or high temperatures (200 °C). A full factorial design was applied to obtain the most efficient synthesis conditions.BSQDs displayed excitation-independent emissions, demonstrating the purity of the synthesized BSQDs, with a maximum fluorescence at 425 nm after excitation at 310 nm. Eltrombopag olamine is an anti-thrombocytopenia drug that is also reported to cause toxicity in river water based on its Persistence, Bioaccumulation, and Toxicity (PBT). The synthesized BSQDs were employed as the first fluorometric sensor for environmental and bioanalysis of eltrombopag. The fluorescence of BSQDs decreased with increasing concentrations of eltrombopag, with excellent selectivity and sensitivity down to 30 ppb. BSQDs were successfully applied as sensing probes for the detection of eltrombopag in medical tablets, spiked and real human plasma samples, and river water samples, with an overall recovery of at least 97 %. The good tolerance to high levels of foreign components and co-administered drugs indicates good selectivity and versatility of the proposed methodology. Plasma pharmacokinetic parameters such as t1/2, Cmax, and t max of eltrombopag were evaluated to be 9.91 h, 16.0 μg mL−1, and 5 h, respectively. Moreover, the green character of the BSQDs as a sensor was proved by various analytical greenness scales.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Black seed
Microwave irradiation
Carbon quantum dots
Eltrombopag
Pharmacokinetic assay, Environmental assays

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of ELT in spiked and real human plasma
Determination of ELT in river water
QbD strategy and microwave-assisted synthesis of plant-based BSQDs
Morphology, structure, and optical characterization of BSQDs
Sensing of ELT by BSQDs and insights into mechanism
Analysis of ELT in its pharmaceutical samples
Determination of ELT in spiked and real human plasma
Determination of ELT in river water samples
Comparison of the performance of the BSQDs synthesis method and analytical method with the literature

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of BSQDs QY with that prepared using sucrose and a mixture of sucrose and black seed.
The 23 full factorial design main effect plots (A), full interaction plots (B), optimization plots for the synthesis of BSQDs (C), and Pareto chart of the effects on QY response at α = 0.05(D).

Characterization of the synthesized BSQDs where (A) TEM image of BSQDs and their size distribution (from 5 to 9 nm), (B) EDX spectrum of as-synthesized BSQDs, (C) FT-IR spectrum of BSQDs. (D) shows the elemental mapping of BSQDs, (E) a co-plot showing great overlap between UV–Vis absorption spectrum of ELT [7.0 μg/mL] and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of BSQDs (a, a‘) (inset: BSQDs at normal light(left) and BSQDs at UV light(right)), (F) Fluorescence emission spectra of BSQDs at 425 nm with different excitation wavelengths.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of BSQDs in aqueous solution upon the addition of various concentrations of ELT (from top to bottom: 0 μg/mL, 0.1 μg/mL, 1.0 μg/mL, 3.0 μg/mL, 5.0 μg/mL, 7.0 μg/mL, 9.0 μg/mL, 12.0 μg/mL), (B) Linear regression plot of the decrease in fluorescence intensity and ELTconcentration in the range of 0.1–12.0 μg/mL, and (C) Suppressed efficiency of the observed and corrected fluorescence of BSQDs after the addition of different concentrations of ELT.

(A) Selectivity and interference of BSQDs towards ELT in the presence of other common species. The fluorescence intensities of BSQDs without and with ELT at 425 nm, respectively, and (B) mean plasma concentration–time profile of eltrombopag in human plasma following oral dosing of eltrombopag (50 mg tablet) to a human volunteer.
General procedure for synthesis and application of BSQDs.
The 23 experimental full factorial design (23 FFD) and their dependent response for synthesis of BSQDs.
Effect of co-administered drugs on the proposed spectrofluorometric method for determination of ELT.
Application of the proposed fluorescence sensor for ELT determination in spiked human plasma and river water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each result is the average of three separate determinations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the adopted synthesis approach of BSQDs with that in the literature and analytical figures of merits of BSQDs as nanosensors for ELT.

row.entry: Commercial tablets, river water, and spiked and real human plasma

head.ce:title: Quality by design−aided acid-free synthesis of self P, N, S-doped black seed-derived carbon quantum dots for application as a nanosensor for eltrombopag environmental and bioanalysis and pharmacokinetic assay

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, 35516, Mansoura, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, 35516, Mansoura, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry for Pharmaceuticals, Course of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences, Nagasaki University, 1-14 Bunkyo-machi, Nagasaki, 852-8521, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry for Pharmaceuticals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry for Pharmaceuticals, Course of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences, Nagasaki University, 1-14 Bunkyo-machi, Nagasaki, 852-8521, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we developed a rapid, one-step, and cost-effective methodology based on the fabrication of water-soluble self-nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus co-doped black seed carbon quantum dots (BSQDs) via microwaveirradiation in six minutes. Our synthesis approach is superior to those in the literature as they involved long-time heating (12 h) with sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide and/or high temperatures (200 °C). A full factorial design was applied to obtain the most efficient synthesis conditions.BSQDs displayed excitation-independent emissions, demonstrating the purity of the synthesized BSQDs, with a maximum fluorescence at 425 nm after excitation at 310 nm. Eltrombopag olamine is an anti-thrombocytopenia drug that is also reported to cause toxicity in river water based on its Persistence, Bioaccumulation, and Toxicity (PBT). The synthesized BSQDs were employed as the first fluorometric sensor for environmental and bioanalysis of eltrombopag. The fluorescence of BSQDs decreased with increasing concentrations of eltrombopag, with excellent selectivity and sensitivity down to 30 ppb. BSQDs were successfully applied as sensing probes for the detection of eltrombopag in medical tablets, spiked and real human plasma samples, and river water samples, with an overall recovery of at least 97 %. The good tolerance to high levels of foreign components and co-administered drugs indicates good selectivity and versatility of the proposed methodology. Plasma pharmacokinetic parameters such as t1/2, Cmax, and t max of eltrombopag were evaluated to be 9.91 h, 16.0 μg mL−1, and 5 h, respectively. Moreover, the green character of the BSQDs as a sensor was proved by various analytical greenness scales.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Among the attractive materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a class of carbon nanomaterial with unique tunable optoelectronic properties, have received increasing attention and multiple distinctive applications in a diversity of areas. CQDs possess several superior and exceptional merits, including long photostability, controllable photoluminescence, good water solubility, robust chemical inertness, eco-friendliness, convenient preparation, surface modification, low cost, and low toxicity [1,2]. They are widely applied for tremendous biomedical applications, including biosensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, and photodynamic and cancer therapy, ensuring their safety in human contact applications [3,4]. Along with their potential biological features, specific recent studies have reported potent antioxidant and strong antibacterial activity of CQDs. With efficient doping and further surface modification through the incorporation of heteroatoms to improve the optical properties, CQDs-based fluorescent sensors are thus far able to quantify various compounds, including metals and small bioactive molecules [5,6]. Flexible functionalized nanomaterials have been synthesized and incorporated into the development of the analytical method for the analysis of different chemical species by acting as optical probes [7–9].

Our research group has been able to fabricate new carbon quantum dots from different sources to facilitate the determination of different analytes [10–14]. Several analytical techniques were applied, including spectrofluorimetry and a smartphone-based reader [2,15,16]. We recently sought to increase the greenness of the prepared quantum dots by using natural precursors.

The seeds of Nigella sativa, usually known as black cumin seed or black seed, have been used as food and traditional medicine by many Asian, Middle Eastern, and Far Eastern Countries. Proximate analysis of whole mature Nigella seeds showed that the moisture content ranged from 5.52 % to 7.43 %, crude protein from 20 % to 27 %, ash from 3.77 % to 4.92 %, carbohydrates from 23.5 % to 33.2 % and ether-extractable lipid from 34.49 % to 38.72 %. Potassium, phosphorus, sodium, and iron were the predominant elements present. Zinc, calcium, magnesium, manganese, and copper were found at lower levels [17,18].It is an annual herb with several pharmacological effects, including treatment of diarrhea,abdominal pain, asthma, coughs, rheumatism, headache, and other diseases. It is also characterized by its anti-cancer, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory activities in relation to its flavonoid components [19]. Black seed was utilized to synthesize black seed QDs (BSQDs) by hydrothermal method for 12 h at 120 °C with a low quantum yield (QY) of 8 % [20] or for 4 h at 200 °C with QY of 7.2 % [21]. It was also used to fabricate quantum dots by both hydrothermal and pyrolysis methods with QY of 7 % and 2 %, respectively [22]. The hydrothermal method was also used to obtain CQDs from black seed using 0.5 M sulfuric acid solution for 4 h at 200 °C with QY of 2 % [23]. To overcome low QY, long application of high temperatures, and difficulty of BSQDs synthesis of the previously reported methods, we use only the seeds without the addition of any chemical substances and expose the seeds to microwave irradiation to reduce synthesis time and achieve simplicity, greenness, efficiency, and speed to obtain CQDs with high QY.

The quality by design (QbD) toolis used to implement the design of experiments (DoE). It is an effective, systematic, risk-controlled, less time-consuming, and knowledge-based method aimed at initial design to optimize the manufacturing process. Furthermore, it supplies huge amounts of information from the least number of trials by systematically modifying the factors and thus can evaluate the effects of multiple variables simultaneously [24,25].

Idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) is characterized by a decreased platelet count, which results from deficient platelet production and increased platelet destruction. Even though the development of autoantibodies against platelet glycoproteins remains central in the pathophysiology of ITP, numerous abnormalities have been identified, including cellular mechanisms to modulate immunity [26].

Eltrombopag olamine is the orally active ethanolamine salt ofeltrombopag, which is a red-brown crystalline solid that is chemically 2-aminoethanol;3-[3-[[2-(3,4-dimethylphenyl)-5-methyl-3-oxo-1H-pyrazol-4-yl]diazenyl]-2-hydroxyphenyl]benzoic acid. It was used for the treatment of thrombocytopenia in patients with chronic immune ITP who had an insufficient response to immunoglobulin therapy, corticosteroids, or splenectomy [27]. To date, several automated approaches and analytical techniques have been developed to estimate ELT. For instance, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [28–32], ultra-performance liquid chromatographic (UPLC) [33], and voltammetry [34].

The preparation of carbon dots from natural sources and cheap precursors has added remarkable advantages to the analysis methods; hence, we synthesized BSQDs with blue fluorescence from black seed without the use of any chemical additives by using a household microwave. A complete evaluation of the structural, optical, and physiological properties of the green synthesized BSQDs was conducted and discussed. BSQDs were applied for sensitive and rapid detection of eltrombopag olamine (ELT) based on suppression of the fluorescence intensity of the dots. The mechanism of quenching was attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE) phenomenon. IFE cansignificantly convert changes in absorption values into changes in fluorescence intensity, which provides higher sensitivity. IFE-based fluorescence assay indicates the apparent spectral overlap of fluorophore’s emission/excitation with quencher’s absorption spectrum [35–37]. The prepared sensor was employed for selective assay of ELT in commercial tablets and human plasma samples without costly or hazardous reagents, with substantial yield and significant validation compliance. Moreover, the developed method could follow ELT pharmacokinetic behavior in real human plasma samples after administration of its tablets. Besides, the method was applied to determine ELT in river water samples as an emerging contaminant owing to its persistence, bioaccumulation, and toxicity (PBT) possibility, as well as the screening of sunlight. It also causes toxicity to algae and fish [38].

The developed study demonstrates no derivatization, extraction, heating, or equilibration. Thus, the proposed system exhibited obvious workup simplicity. The greenness profiles of the proposed method were assessed using ECO-scale, complex-GAPI, AGREEprep, and AGREE metrics.

Nigella Sativa was obtained from a local store in Mansoura city, Egypt. Eltrombopag olamine standard was obtained from Amoun, Pharmaceutical Co., Cairo, Egypt. Revolade® Tablets (Batch No. 91406026003403) and Versapenia® Tablets (Batch No. 226757) containing 50 mg ELT/ tablet, a product of Glaxo Smithkline and Amoun Pharmaceutical Co., Cairo, Egypt, respectively, were bought from local Pharmacy. L-lysine, L-histidine, L-threonine, L-tryptophan, citric acid, lactose, glucose, quinine sulfate (99.0 %), acetic acid (96%), phosphoric acid, boric acid, sodium hydroxide, zinc powder, and metal salts (sodium chloride, potassium chloride, potassium acetate, calcium chloride, magnesium chloride, nickel chloride, cobalt (II) chloride, and cadmium sulfate) were obtained from Sigma − Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany. Human plasma samples were obtained from Mansoura University Hospitals [Mansoura, Egypt] and kept frozen until needed after gentle thawing. Real water samples used in this study were collected locally from the Nile River in Mansoura City, Egypt. For all experiments, all solutions were prepared from ultra-pure water, and all chemicals were of Analytical Reagent Grade, purchased from commercial suppliers, and no further purification was required.

Britton Robinson buffers (pH 2.0–12.0) were used during the study. Briefly, it was prepared using acids, including boric, acetic, and phosphoric acids (each of 0.04 M). The pH of this combination was adjusted to the desired value through 0.2 M NaOH. A final stock solution of ELT (100.0 µg/mL) was obtained by dissolving accurately weighed 10 mg of pure drug powder in 100 mL methanol.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were conducted on a UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu-1601) and a spectrofluorometer (Cary Eclipse, Santa Clara, CA 95,051, United States), respectively. The fluorescence spectrophotometer is equipped witha Xenon flash lamp used at 800 V. The slit width for the emission and excitation measurements was 5 nm.Surface functional groups of the prepared samples were identified by operating (IS10 Nicolet, USA) Fourier-Transform Infrared spectrophotometer (FT-IR) in the rangeof 400 to 4000 cm−1 using the KBr method. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy was conducted using EDX, Oxford-Xmax 20. A Smart R-750MR(K) microwave (900 W-2450 MHz) was used for the preparation of BSQDs. A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) was utilized to study the size and shape of BSQDs (JEOL, JSM −2100-Tokyo, Japan) operated at 200 kV acceleration voltage. The factorial design was statistically performed using Minitab® Statistical Software (Release 16, State College, Pennsylvania, USA).

BSQDs were synthesized using a domestic microwave and black seeds (nigella sativa plant) as a precursor. In brief, black seeds were finely powdered by home miller, and 2.0 g of the powder was suspended in 3 mL of ultra-pure water. The mixture was transferred into a beaker and irradiatedin a microwave for 6 min at 900 W. The obtained black dry residue was then sonicated with 20 mL of ultra-pure water for 15 min and then filtered using Whatman filter paper with a pore size of 0.2 µm. The resulting filtrate was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min to remove large particles. The resultant solution was filtered through a 0.45 µm disk filter. The pale yellow solution was stored in an amber-colored bottle in the refrigerator at 4 °C for further use. A stock solution of BSQDs was prepared by transferring 4 mL of the pale yellow synthesized dots into a 100 mL volumetric flask and completing it up to the mark with ultra-pure water. Both working solutions were further stored in the refrigerator and were found to be stable for one month. The full procedure applied for the synthesis of BSQDs fromthe nigella sativa plant and its sensing applications is demonstrated in Scheme 1
. Quantum yield (QY) measurement of the as-prepared dots was supplied in supplementary material S.1 .

In a typical procedure, 500 µL aliquots from the standard solution of ELT (100.0 µg/mL) over the range (0.10 to 12.0 µg/mL) were sequentially added into a cuvette containing 200 µL stock BSQDs and 1.3 mL water. The emission spectra were then recorded using 310 nm excitation wavelength at room temperature. A blank sample was prepared to determine the relative fluorescence intensity (RFI). In order to deduce the regression equation, a graph between the decrease in the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm (ΔF = F0 − F) and ELT concentrations (µg/mL) was plotted to obtain the calibration curve. Finally, the selectivity of BSQDs for ELT over other compounds was examined by adding various interfering substances to the BSQDs-ELT mixture, and subsequent changes in photoluminescence (PL) intensity were clearly plotted. The procedure for analysis of ELT in commercial tablets (Revolade® and Versapenia® tablets) was supplied under supplementary Section S.2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ELT in spiked and real human plasma
ce:section.ce:para: A 500 µL aliquots of human plasma with different increasing concentrations of ELT were transferred into 15 mL centrifugation tubes and vortexer-mixed for 2 min; the volume was completed up to 2 mL with methanol. The tubes were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min to ensure phase separation. A 500 µL aliquots of the supernatant were filtered and transferred into a cuvette. The procedure mentioned in Section “2.4” was followed, and a blank plasma sample was also prepared. The percent recovery for each concentration of ELT was determined through the regression equation derived from plotting the data of ΔF and ELT concentration (µg/mL) in plasma.

For the assay of ELT in real human plasma, one tablet of Revolade® formulation containing 50 mg ELT was taken orally by a fasting volunteer (a woman around 45 years old). Approval of the Ethics Committee in the Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura, Egypt, was obtained, and the study was registered under (Protocol Code −2023–221). Further, 2 mL blood samples were drawn at time intervals at 3, 5, 7, 12, and 17 h into tubes with 0.5 ml of 2 % EDTA solution to avoid coagulation. The blood was converted to plasma by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant plasma samples were carefully transferred into test tubes, and the procedure described under Section “2.4” was applied. The plasma level of the drug was calculated using the corresponding regression equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ELT in river water
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the feasibility of the current method, river water samples were collected from the banks of the Nile River at Mansoura City, Egypt, applying the water sampling protocols. The water samples were initially exposed to centrifugation at 6000 RPM for 20 min, then filtered and spiked with ELT representing several concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 12.0 µg/mL. Next, 500 µL aliquots of the spiked river water samples were treated as in the general procedure under section “2.4” in parallel with a blank experiment to obtain the recovery % of ELT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: QbD strategy and microwave-assisted synthesis of plant-based BSQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, BSQDs were synthesized based on green chemistry principles. The microwave-assisted method for QDs production has numerous advantages, involving uniform circulation of energy within the reaction container, minimal reaction time, energy conservation, environmental benignity, high reproducibility, and exceptional control over experimental limits. Several different materials have been used alone or in combination to fabricate CQDs with different properties and functions. The use of sucrose and black seed as a precursor for carbon dots may represent an environment-friendly way of synthesis that takes advantage of one of the most available, non-toxic, and cheap raw materials [39]. Initially, the quantum yield efficiency was investigated using sucrose, black seed, and a combination of sucrose and black seed in a (1:1) ratio while keeping the microwave irradiation time, microwave power, and the volume of supplemented water constant, as shown in Fig. 1 . The highest quantum yield was obtained by utilizing black seed only as a source of quantum dots based on its higher carbohydrate, protein, and phosphorus content [18].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, structure, and optical characterization of BSQDs
ce:section.ce:para: For careful observation, the nanostructure ofBSQDs could be examined using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM). The pattern (Fig. 3 A) showed that the as-prepared dots had well-distributed particles with circular geometry and particle size distribution between 5 and 9 nm. In addition, energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy was employed to investigate the elemental composition of the prepared dots. Fig. 3B depicts the EDX spectra of BSQDs, confirming the existence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, calcium, potassium, and phosphorus in the sample [17]. Furthermore, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra measurements of BSQDs were also conducted to better understand their structure, elemental composition, and surficial functional groups. As shown in Fig. 3C, characteristic peaks centered at 579 and 2932 cm−1 correspond to C–H, while a strong peak at 1624 cm−1 is attributed to C = C. Comparatively, the bands at 1300, 675, 1711, and 2200 cm−1 were assigned to P = O, C-P [40], C = O, and C–N, respectively. Whereas a broad peak at 3380 cm−1 is associated with O–H and N–H stretching vibrations. The broad diffraction peaks demonstrate the production of BSQDs of smallcrystals. Fig. 3D shows the EDX elemental mapping images that illustrate the signals of C, N, S, and O elements. Besides, all the elements are uniformly distributed in BSQDs.The prepared dots have yellowish-brown color under normal light but show blue fluorescence under UV light of 365 nm, as in Fig. 3E. BSQDs exhibit a constant emission band centered at 425 nm with different intensities, although changing the excitation wavelength from 300-360 nm, confirming that the dots display excitation-independent emission behavior, as indicated in Fig. 3F. Excitation independence ensures a stable fluorescence emission and perfect optical properties of BSQDs, which might be a result of the monotonous surface defeats from the uniform preparation conditions and heteroatom dopant, indicating the homogeneity and good purity of the as-prepared BSQDs [41]. The as-prepared dots displayed strong blue-luminescent behavior at 425 nm upon excitation at 310 nm (Fig. 3E). The QY of the synthesized BSQDs was calculated as mentioned in the experimental section S1 and was found to be 13.2 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of ELT by BSQDs and insights into mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
Using the optimal experimental conditions, we investigated the response of BSQDs towards ELT. In Fig. 4 A, with the addition of increasing ELT concentrations, the fluorescence intensity of BSQDs at 425 nm was quenched gradually. The ΔF is linear to ELT concentrations from 0.1 to 12.0 μg/mL (Fig. 4B). Generally, the up-to-date quenching mechanism of fluorescent sensors mainly includes dynamic quenching, static quenching, intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoelectron transfer (PET), and IFE [42]. Herein, there is a spectral overlap between the absorption band of the absorbent (ELT) and excitation and emission bands of the fluorophore (BSQDs), as demonstrated in Fig. 2E. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of BSQDs by ELT may be explained by IFE. The emission intensity of BSQDs was then corrected after adding increasing concentrations of ELT for potential IFE according to the following equation [10,35]:
(2)
F
Corr
=
F
Obs
X
10
A
Ex
+
A
Em
2
Where FObs and FCorr are the measured and corrected fluorescence intensities, respectively. AEm and AEx refer to the absorbance at the maximum emission wavelength (425 nm) and the maximum excitation wavelength (310 nm), respectively.

Subsequently, the suppressed efficiency (%E) for both observed and corrected fluorescence could be obtained from the following equation (3):
(3)
% E =
1 -
F
F
O
x 100

Plotting the relationship of %E of each observed and corrected fluorescence intensities for BSQDs versus ELT concentrations in µg/mL (Fig. 4C) confirmed that the interaction between ELT and BSQDs occurred mainly through secondary IFE.

Variable parameters were evaluated in the presence of ELT as a model analyte aiming at the highest fluorescence signal. In order to investigate the stability of the established methodology, both pH and incubation time effects on the fluorescence intensity were examined. The studied Britton Robinson buffer at pH range (2.1–12) in Supplementary Fig. S1A. reveals a significant effect of pH on the emission intensity. ELT has five protonatable sites in the pH range of 2–10 [43], which resulted in a non-uniform pattern during the study, as shown in Supplementary Fig. S1A. However, it was found that, without using a buffer, the quenching efficiency was maximum. Hence, no buffer was used in the measurement procedure. Next, continuous variation of incubation time starting from 1 to 20 min was studied, and it was found that the PL intensity remained unchanged, as shown in Supplementary Fig. S1B. The findings indicated that the reaction between ELT and the dots occurred immediately and persisted in a stable state for at least 20 min, which was further proof of the IFE quenching mechanism.

An evaluation of the established methodology was done regarding ICH Q2R1 recommendations [44]. The PL intensity of BSQDs significantly diminishes with increasing ELT concentration, which indicates that the emission intensity of the as-prepared dots is sensitive to ELT concentration. A good linear correlation is detected underneath the concentration array from 0.10 to 12.0 μg/mL. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ) are assessed to be 0.030 μg/mL and 0.091 μg/mL, respectively, as illustrated in Supplementary Table S1.

The method's accuracy was tested by comparing the analysis results of the designed approach with those obtained using the comparison method (HPLC was performed using a C18 column, 25 % ammonium formate, and 75 % acetonitrile as a mobile phase, at a flow rate 1.0 mL/min with a detection wavelength of 230 nm [32]. High recovery percentages were noticed, reflecting the high performance of the method with respect to precision and accuracy using both the Student t-test and Variance ratio F-test [45] (Supplementary Table S2).

The specificity of the suggested fluorimetric method was monitored, confirming that no intrusions were observed from the excipients and the additives of (Revolade® and Versapenia® tablets) during the determination of ELT (Supplementary Table S4 ).

Selectivity is an essential issue that must be considered to ensure the efficiency of the prepared sensor. Therefore, we investigated the variation in the fluorescence intensity of BSQDs by the addition of different interfering co-administered drugs (dexamethasone, tranexamic acid, and vitamin K), biological molecules including (L-lysine, L-histidine, L-threonine, L-tryptophan, citric acid, lactose, and glucose), anions (CH3COO–, and Cl-) and metal ions (K+, Mg2 +, Na+, Cd2 +, Zn2 +, Co2 +, and Ni2 +), which could be present in the analyzed matrices, to fixed volume of the dots (100.0 μL) either in the presence or in the absence of ELT (5.0 μg mL−1 ). Numerous solutions of metal ions were prepared by adding corresponding metal salts to ultra-pure water and sonicating it for 10 min. Higher tolerance limit values were obtained for co-administrated drugs (dexamethasone, tranexamic acid, and vitamin K), reflecting the remarkable selectivity of the designed system (Table 2
). From Fig. 5 A, it is evidently observed that except ELT, all interfering substances and ions have an insignificant impact on the emission intensity of BSQDs. Therefore, BSQDs exhibited good selectivity for ELT detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ELT in its pharmaceutical samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of BSQDs as bio-sensing fluorescent probes was verified by the recovery assay of ELT in its commercial tablets. The experimental results were summarized in (Supplementary Table S4 ) demonstrating the ability of the method to detect ELT in its commercial forms without potential interference from any additives or excipients according to high recovery rates between 99.33 % and 101.0 %. Perfect coordination is definitely achieved between the proposed and HPLC reported method [32] through Student's t-test and Variance Ratio F-test [45]. Our designed system is superior to the HPLC-reported method in that it does not require the consumption of large volumes of solvents and has the same reliability, accuracy, precision, and higher sensitivity than the comparison and reference methods of different techniques rather than spectrofluorimetry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ELT in spiked and real human plasma
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of the developed approach allowed its application for ELT estimation in human plasma within a low range of 0.1–7.0 μg mL−1 (Table 3 ). The extraction process was performed using simple protein precipitation and centrifugation steps. The response attained by the developed methodology has a linear equation:

Furthermore, the application of the method was extended to the in-vivo estimation of ELT in human subjects following administration of a single dose of Revolade® (50 mg ELT/tablet) orally by a healthy volunteer. The pharmacokinetics of ELT were studied and monitored, and the maximum plasma concentration (Cmax) was found to be 16 μg mL−1 at tmax of 5 h (Fig. 5B). These results were found to be in agreement with previously reported results [7]. The estimated half-life of ELT (t1/2) was 9.91 h, which is close to that of the reported method [7].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ELT in river water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Eltrombopag has been reported to cause toxicity in river water based on its Persistence, Bioaccumulation, and Toxicity (PBT), resulting in significant toxicity to aquatic species, especially fish [38]. Hence, this sensing method was also applied to verify the utilization of BSQDs as the turn-off probe for ELT estimation in river water samples for the first time. To check the application for BSQDs in real water samples, different concentrations of ELT were spiked to the river water samples (Table 3) and analyzed using BSQDs, and fluorescence spectra were recorded. The results in Table 3 show that the proposed method has an acceptable accuracy, with % recoveries ranging from 98.0 % to 102.2 %.

Analytical methods involve many chemicals and organic solvents that may pose hazards to the environment, so it’s necessary to evaluate the greenness of the method. In this study, we used different approaches to assess the greenness of our developed method. Firstly, ECO-scale was applied to assay the greenness of the analytical procedures. It is based on the estimation of the procedure's penalty points, which are then subtracted from the “ideal green analysis” (100). The ECO-scale score was determined for the method and compared with “ideal green analysis”. A method could be described as an outstanding green analysis when the overall score is more than 75 [46]. The scale value was found to be 94 as the solvent used in this method is water, and there is an absence of energy and heat-consuming methods. Therefore, the method shows a high degree of greenness (Supplementary Table S5).

Moreover, the complex green analytical procedure index (Complex-GAPI) is a tool that includes all multidisciplinary aspects of an experimental process, starting from sample sources, collection, transport, preservation, and storage to final estimation. It also relates to all experimental steps performed prior to sample analysis, such as the synthesis of nanoparticles. Generally, it is five pentagrams representing sample assembly and preparation, solvents and reagents, method type, and instrumentation, with an additional hexagonal field for greenness estimation of the pre-analysis processes. Greenness rating is based on a color scale, with green, yellow, and red reflecting eco-friendly, moderate, and hazardous environmental impact, respectively [47,48]. The evaluation results are displayed in Supplementary Fig. S2A.

Recently, a metric tool, namely AGREEprep, was developed to assess the environmental impact of sample preparation.It is applied to evaluate the utilized solvents and reagents, sample size, energy consumption, waste production, and throughput. The obtained result is a colorful round pictogram with the number in the center in the range from 0 to 1, with 1 equivalent to the best performance. The greenness performance in sample preparation is generally demonstrated by both the intrinsic value and the color of the inner circle. The inner circle is surrounded by 10 segments, each of which reflects one of the ten performance criteria. Most of these principles are mainly associated with sample preparation, but two principles (1 and 9) relate to the analytical activities of pre- and post-sample preparation. The part color corresponds to the performance in the assessment criterion, while its length reflects the weight assigned [49,50]. The results of the AGREEprep evaluations of the developed method are presented in Supplementary Fig. S2B.

AGREE is another semi-quantitative approach with a 12-segment clock diagram. Each section represents one of 12 green analytical principles. The score is 0.84, which is displayed in the center of the chart and reflects less waste, lower energy technique, and simplicity, thus confirming the greenness behavior of the method, as indicated by the central green color in Supplementary Fig. S2C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the performance of the BSQDs synthesis method and analytical method with the literature
ce:section.ce:para:
A comparison of the synthesized BSQDs QY with previous reports using black seed as a precursor is listed in Table 4
. It is clear that the QY of BSQDs synthesized using a microwave is 1.7 to 7 times higher than those synthesized by other methods, which were laborious and time-consuming. Moreover, the microwave synthesis time was 40 to 120 times faster than all previously reported BSQDs hydrothermal and pyrolysis methods. Compared with previously reported methods, the established analytical approach offers higher sensitivity, as indicated in Table 4. Our method was 30 to 2500 times more sensitive than the HPLC [32] and UHPLC [33] methods. On the other hand, the method showed sensitivity similar to the voltammetric [34] and LC-MS [28] methods; however, these methods require experienced analysis and expensive instrumentation. It is clear that the microwave synthesis method of BSQDs is more advantageous than previously reported literature in terms of QY, synthesis time, and facile procedure. Additionally, the developed sensor and the simple analytical method showed very good sensitivity, which is better than or comparable to the literature.

A facile, high-throughput, and cost-effective microwave-assisted approach was developed for synthesizing BSQDs from black seeds (nigella sativa seeds) serving as a precursor and N, S, and P-dopant by exposure to microwave radiation for 6 min. A QbD approach was applied during the preparation of the CDs from black seeds using a household microwave. The BSQDs display bright blue luminescence at 425 nm after excitation at 310 nm with a QY of 13.7 %. Subsequently, the as-prepared BSQDs were used as highly sensitive sensors in order to estimate and monitor ELT in aqueous solutions, commercial tablets, spiked, and real human plasma and extended to river water samples and pharmacokinetic assay with high sensitivity. It is the first spectrofluorimetric method for the determination of ELT in different matrices. The PL quenching behavior of BSQDs by ELT was very selective to the targeted drug. The greenness of the method was assessed through various metrics and scales, and the method was found to be compatible with the concepts of green analysis.
Funding:
This work is funded by Mansoura University (project code number: Mu-phar-23–32).

Shymaa M. Abd Elhaleem: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Fathalla Belal: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yasser El-Shabrawy: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation. Mahmoud El-Maghrabey: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124495.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
